text:
That quote reminds me of the memory palace / <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Method_of_loci|method of loci technique> used in speed memorization contests. :nerd_face:
Ryan Singer also talks about this <https://synthetic.transistor.fm/episodes/design-as-a-multi-scale-network-thin-vs-fat-tailed-problems|idea of Multi-scale networks> to solve problems (though in a slightly different context)

I think we already do something like this in programming today:
A `for loop` is an abstraction for a set of assembly code with an increment operation, a comparison operation, and a jump operation.
A function or method is an abstraction for a set of logically related instructions.
A module or API is a an abstraction that represents a set of related functions.
edit: Additionally, a DSL or a `macro` is somewhere between a function and an API. I haven't decided which yet. :stuck_out_tongue:

text:
Hi! I have created a snapshot version 0.6.0 for Operon which is the basis for the first "official" release. Find it from <https://operon.io|https://operon.io>.

"Operon is a programming language which can be easily embedded with JVM-based languages (Java, Scala, Groovy, Kotlin, Clojure, etc.) or run standalone with the native-version. Operon is especially great for transforming and handling JSON-data. At first sight it appears to be a kind of query-language, but it is actually a functional programming language that has clear syntax and semantics and can be used for many purposes (e.g. microservices, integrations) and even to build larger solutions."

Operon is licensed under Operon-license.

Please give it a test-drive and let me hear back from you :blush:

Please give it a test-drive and let me hear back from you

text:
Wow! Really really cool ideas here.

What I like:
• Use of 2D to simply organize your work. In the end, your work is in some kind of map, that you have build and that you can visually remember. 
• Use of links only for event triggers.
• In fact it is kind of similar to traditional nodes'n wire environments except as there is no text by default in nodes, you can look to the patch from any angle, and it is the point of view of the user that defines the text orientation when you need to display it.
• To increase the volume parameter for a node you drag'n drop new "volume+" cells on the node and the volume will be higher if there are more cells. Same with other parameters.

elements.text:
Wow! Really really cool ideas here.

What I like:
Use of 2D to simply organize your work. In the end, your work is in some kind of map, that you have build and that you can visually remember.
Use of links only for event triggers.

In fact it is kind of similar to traditional nodes'n wire environments except as there is no text by default in nodes, you can look to the patch from any angle, and it is the point of view of the user that defines the text orientation when you need to display it.
To increase the volume parameter for a node you drag'n drop new "volume+" cells on the node and the volume will be higher if there are more cells. Same with other parameters.

text:
When I did this at invision, we leveraged the greensock API for our motion prototyping tool
The barrier for most devs seems to be understanding the three animation cases (transition, add, and delete), handling the corner cases (usually things like a half-drag, undo/redo/back/forward, and click during transition), and dealing with topology morphing (square-> circle or solid->gradient, though there are some decent libraries for this)

elements.text: When I did this at invision, we leveraged the greensock API for our motion prototyping tool

The barrier for most devs seems to be understanding the three animation cases (transition, add, and delete), handling the corner cases (usually things like a half-drag, undo/redo/back/forward, and click during transition), and dealing with topology morphing (square-> circle or solid->gradient, though there are some decent libraries for this)

text: I’ll also say from experience that designers LOVE to tweak animation timings, so make that easy :)

elements.text: I’ll also say from experience that designers LOVE to tweak animation timings, so make that easy :)
Apparently there will be a Scratch-like visual programming editor in the new Battlefield game. End-user programming for the masses?

One of our biggest goals with Battlefield Portal is to give you the freedom to explore Battlefield your way by letting you create your own Experiences . For those who wish to go beyond the multitude of Settings, Battlefield Portal will also have a Logic Editor that lets players use streamlined visual scripting logic to have even more control when it comes to creating your experiences. While anyone can use the Logic Editor, it’s aimed at those who have prior knowledge of visual scripting logic

. While the Settings will allow you to change many specific conditions with the flick of a switch, the Logic Editor will let you define rules, victory conditions, consequences of specific in-game events (like setting a reward or punishment for a player scoring a kill), and even more to create custom game modes that aren’t possible to create with the Settings alone.
https://www.ea.com/en-gb/games/battlefield/battlefield-2042/news/battlefield-briefing-exploring-battlefield-portal

text: I thought about Hungarian notation as well, but I don't think it is similar. The prefix tells you the logical or physical type of a variable, but it doesn't tell you the semantics, it doesn't fully define it.

elements.text: I thought about Hungarian notation as well, but I don't think it is similar. The prefix tells you the logical or physical type of a variable, but it doesn't tell you the semantics, it doesn't fully define it.

text: When I work on a large project, I accumulate a list of common words that describe recurring concepts, and join these together like morphemes to form identifiers. Lots of software projects do this, but the "morphemes" tend to be project specific, and don't tend to be particularly standardized. Although you can reuse morphemes from the standard library of the programming language you are using as your base.

elements.text: When I work on a large project, I accumulate a list of common words that describe recurring concepts, and join these together like morphemes to form identifiers. Lots of software projects do this, but the "morphemes" tend to be project specific, and don't tend to be particularly standardized. Although you can reuse morphemes from the standard library of the programming language you are using as your base.

text: IUPAC names use fully standardized "morphemes" to fully describe chemical compounds. The closest I can think of to that situation is languages in the APL family (APL, J, K) where clusters of verbs and adverbs (single characters in APL and K) are used as idioms to denote various useful functions. These character clusters are the same length as library function names in conventional programming languages, but they fully describe the semantics of the function.

elements.text: IUPAC names use fully standardized "morphemes" to fully describe chemical compounds. The closest I can think of to that situation is languages in the APL family (APL, J, K) where clusters of verbs and adverbs (single characters in APL and K) are used as idioms to denote various useful functions. These character clusters are the same length as library function names in conventional programming languages, but they fully describe the semantics of the function.

text:
Hungarian notation apparently started out as <@UT60XSVCN> described it, before getting mangled: <https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2005/05/11/making-wrong-code-look-wrong>

It's interesting to think of the tension between Hungarian and type inference. Sometimes you want to minimize how often you mention the 'kind' of something, and other times you want to put it in everywhere.

attachments.title: Making Wrong Code Look Wrong
attachments.text: Way back in September 1983, I started my first real job, working at Oranim, a big bread factory in Israel that made something like 100,000 loaves of bread every night in six giant ovens the size of…
attachments.fallback: Joel on Software: Making Wrong Code Look Wrong

text: Slightly idle thought. I believe that algebra (as in "algebra 101") "calls on the linguistic part of your brain". For example, the linguistic part of your brain can apply the rules of grammar to the sentence "the gostak distims the doshes" to get other sentences like "doshes are things that the gostak can distim". Just as it can rearrange "a * b / c" into "a / c * b"

elements.text: Slightly idle thought. I believe that algebra (as in "algebra 101") "calls on the linguistic part of your brain". For example, the linguistic part of your brain can apply the rules of grammar to the sentence "the gostak distims the doshes" to get other sentences like "doshes are things that the gostak can distim". Just as it can rearrange "a * b / c" into "a / c * b"

text: But, even though the analogy seems fairly direct to me, we are much, much better at the linguistic one. To do all but basic algebra rearrangement, I have to stare at an equation for a while, it doesn't come as naturally as the linguistic example. This is even though I've been doing this kind of thing for a while.
